content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 10, 1990

Suzanne Slupsky
Office of the Registrar of Voters
County of Orange
P.O. Box 11298
Santa Ana, CA 92711

Re: Senate District 31 Election
Our File No. A-90-024

Dear Ms. Slupsky:

This is to confirm telephone advice provided to you on
December 19, 1989, regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1/

A special election has been called for Senate District 31,
which has become vacant because of the retirement of Senator Bill
Campbell. The office became vacant on December 15, 1989. The
primary election date is February 6, 1990, and the runoff election
date is April 10, 1990. You have asked us to provide the filing
deadlines and periods covered by campaign disclosure statements
required to be filed by candidates in the special election and
candidates in the special runoff election.

The filing deadlines and periods covered by campaign
statements required to be filed by candidates being voted upon in
the special election and the special runoff election are set out
in detail in the attachments to this letter. Please note that the
requirements to file pre-election statements are different for
some of the special election candidates, depending on whether they
currently hold state or local elective office, and depending on
whether their currently-held offices are being voted upon in 1990.

_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Suzanne Slupsky
Page Two

The filing schedule in "Attachment 1" applies to:

(1)  Special election candidates who do not currently
     hold a state or local elective office, and

(2)  Special election candidates who hold a local elective
     office, but whose currently-held office is not being
     voted upon in 1990.

The filing schedule in "Attachment 2" applies to:

     Special election candidates who currently hold a state
     or local elective office which is being voted upon in
     June, 1990.

     Please note that the contribution limitations in the Act
apply separately for a fiscal year, for a special election cycle,
and for a special runoff election cycle.  That is, a candidate may
receive contributions from a contributor up to the "fiscal year"
maximum amount, additional contributions up to the maximum amount
during the "special election cycle," and candidates involved in
the special runoff election may accept additional contributions up
to the maximum amount in the "special runoff election cycle."
(See Attachment 3 for definitions of contribution cycles.)  For
example, a candidate who has a campaign bank account to receive
contributions in connection with an election other than the
special election or special election runoff may receive $1,000
from a "person for the "fiscal year," another $1,000 for the
"special election cycle," and, if being voted upon in the special
runoff election, another $1,000 during the "special runoff
election cycle."

     Candidates may transfer contributions from their other
campaign committee accounts into their campaign account for a
special election or for a special runoff election.  However, if
they do so, contributors of the funds transferred cannot make
additional contributions to the candidate that, when added to the
amount transferred, exceed the special or special runoff election
limits.  In addition, campaign funds may not be transferred from a
candidate's special election account or special runoff election
account into the candidate's other campaign accounts if such
transfers would cause the total amount contributed by a single
source to the non-special election account(s) to exceed the
maximum amount for the fiscal year.  "Attachment 3" sets out the
allowable contributions which may be received by candidates being
voted upon in the special election.

Suzanne Slupsky
Page Three


Please refer to the FPPC Interim Information Manual on
Propositions 68 and 73 for detailed information concerning the
contribution limitations. Please refer to the FPPC Information
Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform
Act for information concerning other campaign disclosure
provisions.

If you have additional questions, please call me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

Carla Wardlow
By: Carla Wardlow
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division
